CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Mar-25-2026 Continued To May-13-26 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-20-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'S DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

Defendant Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.'s demurrer to Probate Advance LLC's complaint is overruled.

A demurrer tests whether the challenged cause of action necessarily fails to state a claim for relief. (Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.) In assessing whether the complaint states a cause of action, the court accepts all properly pleaded material facts, but not contentions, deductions, or conclusions of fact or law. (Minton v. Dignity Health (2019) 39 Cal.App.5th 1155, 1161.) The court liberally construes the complaint pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure, sec. 452.

The complaint alleges that Wells Fargo maintained a blocked account pursuant to an order of the probate court (Compl., para. 5), and that the probate court ordered a distribution of $116,100 to Plaintiff from the blocked account (id., para. 7 & Ex. 2). Wells Fargo issued the check to Plaintiff but then permitted the estate administrator, nonparty Dave Toler, to exchange the check to Plaintiff for a check to a different entity that was controlled by Toler. (Id., paras. 8-9.)

Wells Fargo contends that it had no duty to Plaintiff. The Law Firm of Fox & Fox v. Chase Bank, N.A. (2023) 95 Cal.App.5th 182, holds the contrary. In that case, the appellate court concluded that the bank "had a duty to use reasonable care in distributing funds from the blocked account" because the court order of payment from a blocked account created a special relationship and a corresponding duty by the bank. (Id., at p. 197.) Wells Fargo's efforts to distinguish Fox are unpersuasive; it is immaterial for purposes of duty that the Wells Fargo first complied with the court's order and then issued a new check that departed from it.

Wells Fargo further contends that its common law duty is displaced by the California Commercial Code, section 3420, subsection (a). By its terms, this statute applies to actions where the bank makes payment to someone who is not actually a payee on the check. Plaintiff seeks to hold Wells Fargo liable not because it accepted a fraudulent endorsement or because Plaintiff never received the first check but because Wells Fargo issued a check to the wrong payee notwithstanding a court order naming the correct payee. This is outside the fraudulent-endorsement fact pattern of Gil v. Bank of America, N.A. (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 1371, and Lee Newman, M.D., Inc. v. Wells Fargo Bank (2001) 87 Cal.App.4th 73.

proposed order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)
